Base directory for this skill: /Users/felix/.claude/skills/agent-browser

# agent-browser

Fast browser automation CLI for AI agents. Chrome/Chromium via CDP with
accessibility-tree snapshots and compact `@eN` element refs.

Install: `npm i -g agent-browser && agent-browser install`

## Start here

This file is a discovery stub, not the usage guide. Before running any
`agent-browser` command, load the actual workflow content from the CLI:

```bash
agent-browser skills get core             # start here — workflows, common patterns, troubleshooting
agent-browser skills get core --full      # include full command reference and templates
```

The CLI serves skill content that always matches the installed version,
so instructions never go stale. The content in this stub cannot change
between releases, which is why it just points at `skills get core`.

## Specialized skills

Load a specialized skill when the task falls outside browser web pages:

```bash
agent-browser skills get electron          # Electron desktop apps (VS Code, Slack, Discord, Figma, ...)
agent-browser skills get slack             # Slack workspace automation
agent-browser skills get dogfood           # Exploratory testing / QA / bug hunts
agent-browser skills get vercel-sandbox    # agent-browser inside Vercel Sandbox microVMs
agent-browser skills get agentcore         # AWS Bedrock AgentCore cloud browsers
```

Run `agent-browser skills list` to see everything available on the
installed version.

## Why agent-browser

- Fast native Rust CLI, not a Node.js wrapper
- Works with any AI agent (Cursor, Claude Code, Codex, Continue, Windsurf, etc.)
- Chrome/Chromium via CDP with no Playwright or Puppeteer dependency
- Accessibility-tree snapshots with element refs for reliable interaction
- Sessions, authentication vault, state persistence, video recording
- Specialized skills for Electron apps, Slack, exploratory testing, cloud providers

## Observability Dashboard

The dashboard runs independently of browser sessions on port 4848 and can also be opened through a proxied or forwarded URL such as `https://dashboard.agent-browser.localhost`. Agents should stay on the dashboard origin: session tabs, status, and stream traffic are proxied internally, so session ports do not need to be exposed.

## Process model — read this BEFORE you run anything

`agent-browser` is **not** a single process. It is at least three layers,
and confusing them is the #1 source of "why is the window still open?":

| Layer | What it is | Lifetime | Cleanup command |
|-------|------------|----------|-----------------|
| **Daemon** (`agent-browser-darwin-arm64` on macOS) | Long-lived Node process that owns the Chrome for Testing children | Persists across CLI invocations by design — restarting it is slow | `pkill -f "agent-browser-darwin"` |
| **Session** (named handle, e.g. `dashboard-2026-06-06`) | Logical handle inside the daemon: one Chrome for Testing instance + CDP connection | Persists until explicitly closed; survives `agent-browser close` | `agent-browser session close <id>` / `agent-browser session prune` |
| **Tab** (the page inside one session) | What you actually see in a browser window | Dies with `close` | `agent-browser close` |

The most common mistake is treating `agent-browser close` as a full
cleanup. It is not — it closes the *current tab* only. The session
handle and the daemon stay alive. If you switch to a different URL
mid-session, you have just created a tab in a session that will leak.

### Pre-flight check (run before starting any new audit)

```bash
# What is alive right now?
agent-device session list                     # native runner sessions
agent-browser session list                    # Chrome for Testing sessions
lsof -ti:1420                                 # anything on Vite's port
pgrep -lf "tauri-wd\|tauri dev"               # leftover Tauri dev tree
```

If any of those show state from a prior run, decide: do you want a
clean slate, or are you intentionally continuing?

### End-of-task cleanup (run before you stop driving the browser)

Always run **all** of these — `close` alone is not enough:

```bash
agent-browser close                           # close the current tab
agent-browser session prune                   # kill all sessions, not just this one
# Only if you want a full reset (kills the daemon too — slower to restart):
pkill -f "agent-browser-darwin"
```

If you are working inside a Tauri dev tree (which is the case for
every audit of this project), the same pattern applies to the other
surfaces:

```bash
# Stop Tauri dev (npm + node + tauri-wd + rust compiler)
pkill -f "tauri-wd" pkill -f "tauri dev"
lsof -ti:1420 | xargs -r kill -9             # free Vite's port
# Stop agent-device (native runner + daemon)
pkill -f "AgentDeviceRunner"
pkill -f "agent-device/dist/src/internal/daemon.js"
```

### Diagnostic one-liner (paste this when the user asks "what's open?")

```bash
tauri-audit-status() {
  echo "== agent-device sessions =="
  agent-device session list
  echo "== agent-browser sessions =="
  agent-browser session list
  echo "== Tauri processes =="
  pgrep -lf "tauri-wd\|tauri dev" || echo "  (none)"
  echo "== agent-device daemons =="
  pgrep -lf "agent-device.*daemon" || echo "  (none)"
  echo "== agent-browser daemons =="
  pgrep -lf "agent-browser-darwin" || echo "  (none)"
  echo "== Chrome for Testing =="
  pgrep -lf "Google Chrome for Testing" || echo "  (none)"
  echo "== Vite port 1420 =="
  lsof -ti:1420 || echo "  (none)"
  echo "== mcp-server-tauri (should be gone) =="
  pgrep -lf "mcp-server-tauri" || echo "  (none)"
}
```

### Known gotcha: Tauri dev crashes leave `tauri-wd` watchers behind

`npm run tauri dev` spawns a tree of processes (`npm` → `node tauri dev`
→ `tauri-wd` → `cargo`). When the parent dies — crash, `Ctrl-C`, force
quit — the `tauri-wd` watcher often does not. Days later you will see
a `tauri-wd` process with elapsed time matching the day of the crash.
This is harmless but it keeps the workspace under watch. Kill it with
`pkill -f tauri-wd` if you are about to `tauri dev` again from a
clean state.

### Why this matters

A 7-day-old leaked Chrome for Testing window is a user-visible
artifact: the user sees a window that should not be there and assumes
the agent is still running. The "I see an instance open" question is
almost always a leaked session from a prior audit, not a new instance
the agent just started. Diagnose with the one-liner above before
spawning anything new.
